name: Media Use Motivation Scales (MUMS, SNS-motivation, U&G tradition)
core_claim: Media Use Motivation Scales operationalise the Uses & Gratifications (U&G) tradition: rather than asking what a person is like (a stable trait), they ask why a person turns to media in the first place. The foundational instrument is Rubin's Television Viewing Motives Scale (1981/1983), a 27-item measure yielding roughly nine motives: relaxation, companionship, habit, pass-time, entertainment, social interaction, information, arousal, and escape. These motives factor into two higher-order clusters: ritualised viewing (habit, pass-time, companionship, relaxation — using the medium itself diffusely to fill time and regulate mood) and instrumental viewing (information, arousal, entertainment — goal-directed, content-specific seeking). The modern medium-specific descendants are the Watching TV Series Motives Questionnaire (WTSMQ; Flayelle et al., 2019), a 22-item scale with four factors (emotional enhancement, enrichment, coping-escapism, social), and a family of Social Networking Site (SNS) motive scales (SMU-SNS, MOTUS, SUMS-R) measuring entertainment, social connection, information-seeking, self-expression, coping/escapism, pastime, and enhancement. The theoretical link to media preference is more direct than for general personality: a motive is the proximal cause of a viewing episode, whereas a personality trait is a distal disposition that only predicts viewing through the motives it activates. The core thesis behind including this construct is that 'why you watch' (mood-regulation vs. cognitive enrichment vs. social connection vs. escape) sits causally closer to the chosen title than 'who you are', so it should carry sharper genre- and tone-level signal. Crucially, motives also vary WITHIN a person across occasions (the same user wants comfort on Tuesday and intensity on Saturday), making MUMS partly a state-and-context construct, not only a trait — which is both its strength (context sensitivity) and its weakness (instability) for cold-start.
predictive_validity_for_media: Motive-to-content evidence is moderate and genre-cluster-specific rather than title-level. (1) Genre preference from motives (Rubin tradition and successors): arousal-motivated viewers gravitate to dramatic/intense programming while habitual, escapist, and companionship viewers favour comedies and lighter fare; instrumental (information/arousal) motives predict news and reality/factual content, ritualised (habit/pass-time/companionship) motives predict undifferentiated heavy viewing. These are consistent directional findings across decades but reported effect sizes are heterogeneous and often modest. (2) The best single quantitative anchor for the recommendation use case is Costa Pereira & Silveira / the Emotion Gratification Scale study, 'Why Do We Watch? The Role of Emotion Gratifications and Individual Differences in Predicting Rewatchability and Movie Recommendation' (Frontiers in Psychology, 2020, N=318): seven emotion-gratification dimensions (fun, thrill, empathic sadness, release of emotions, social sharing, contemplative experiences, character engagement). Predicting whether a viewer would RECOMMEND a film (R-squared=0.14), the significant standardised betas were social sharing beta=0.13, contemplative experiences beta=0.13, need-for-affect beta=0.12, age beta=-0.16; zero-order r for social sharing-recommendation = 0.25 (p<.001). Predicting REWATCHABILITY (R-squared=0.13): social sharing beta=0.16, thrill beta=0.15, extraversion beta=0.12, age beta=-0.13; rewatch-recommendation r=0.54. (3) Motives correlate modestly with personality (need-for-affect with contemplative r=0.27 and character engagement r=0.24; extraversion with thrill r=0.18 and fun r=0.17; need-for-cognition with contemplative r=0.18), confirming motives carry variance partly distinct from the Big Five. (4) WTSMQ motives (emotional enhancement, enrichment, coping-escapism, social) discriminate viewer subgroups and predict binge-watching engagement, but the WTSMQ literature targets problematic-use outcomes, NOT genre/title preference, so its predictive validity for WHAT to recommend (vs. how much) is largely unestablished. Net: motives predict broad tone/genre clusters and engagement/recommendation propensity with small-to-moderate effects (R-squared around .13-.14, betas .12-.16), comparable to or slightly better than single Big Five traits for emotional-tone outcomes, but they do not predict specific titles and most validation targets consumption volume rather than preference.
instrument_design: Several instruments, varying in length. (1) Rubin Television Viewing Motives Scale: 27 items, 5-point Likert (not at all like me to exactly like me), ~9 subscales of ~3 items each; reliable but long (~3-4 min) and TV-medium framed. (2) WTSMQ (Flayelle et al., 2019): 22 items, Likert, four factors (emotional enhancement, enrichment, coping-escapism, social), validated and the best cross-cultural option (below). (3) Emotion Gratification Scale: 25 items, seven factors. (4) SNS scales: SMU-SNS 27 items / 9 factors; MOTUS (six motives: entertainment, social interaction, information-seeking, instrumental, self-documentation, self-enhancement); SUMS-R 8 factors (information, enhancement, social, coping, pastime, conformity, expression, concealment). For a sub-2-minute, 6-10 item onboarding context, NO off-the-shelf validated short form maps cleanly onto a cross-medium TV+anime recommender. The viable minimum-viable approach is a purpose-built short battery sampling one item per high-value motive: comfort/relaxation, escape, emotional intensity/enhancement, enrichment/contemplation (eudaimonic), social/co-viewing, and information/learning — i.e. 6 items, each anchored to the WTSMQ or Rubin construct it proxies. [uncertain] (no validated 6-10 item cross-medium motive short form was located; building one forfeits the source instruments' validated invariance and would need its own validation). Response format should be media-framed forced-choice or scenario-anchored rather than abstract Likert (see quiz_ux_implications). A critical design caveat absent from trait scales: motive scores partly reflect the user's CURRENT state and recent viewing context, so a single onboarding snapshot captures a blend of stable motive-disposition and transient mood — for cold-start this is usable but should be treated as a soft prior, refined as behaviour accrues.
cold_start_applicability: Moderate-to-good, with a specific advantage and a specific liability versus trait scales. Advantage: motives are proximal to viewing choice, so even a 6-item onboarding battery yields directionally useful signal on the tone/format axis (comfort vs. intensity, escapist vs. enriching, solo vs. social) before any history exists, and that signal maps onto exactly the catalogue dimensions genre tags capture poorly. The Emotion Gratification evidence shows motive dimensions outpredict most single personality traits for recommendation/rewatch propensity (R-squared~.14). Liability: motives are partly state-dependent and context-dependent — a user's escape motive is higher after a stressful week than on a relaxed one — so a one-shot cold-start reading is noisier than a stable trait reading and is better framed as 'what are you usually/right-now in the mood for' than as a fixed profile. Cross-medium transfer (predicting anime tone preference from TV-elicited motives) is theoretically clean because motives are medium-agnostic at the construct level (escape is escape whether the vehicle is a K-drama or a shonen anime), and the WTSMQ/SNS literature shows the same four-to-six motive structure recurs across media — stronger cross-medium grounding than most trait instruments offer. Net: a strong PRIOR on viewing tone/intent, weaker as a stable identity signal; best used to set an initial comfort-vs-intensity and escapist-vs-enriching lean that behaviour then corrects. Pairs naturally with Need for Affect (emotional-intensity appetite) and Need for Cognition (enrichment appetite), which are the trait correlates of two of these motives.
group_compatibility_use: [uncertain] No motive-scale-specific dyadic media-recommendation validation was located, but MUMS has a structural advantage for the HelpME2C two-person case that trait scales lack: the SOCIAL motive is itself a measured dimension (WTSMQ 'social' factor; Emotion Gratification 'social sharing' was the single strongest predictor of both recommendation, beta=0.13, and rewatchability, beta=0.16). This means the instrument directly measures how much each partner views co-watching as the point — which is the joint-watch metric's own currency. For aggregation: (a) the social/co-viewing motive should be a JOINT amplifier, not averaged-down — a pair where both score high on social motive will derive satisfaction from the shared act largely independent of perfect content fit, widening the acceptable recommendation set; (b) the escape/comfort vs. enrichment/intensity axis should aggregate like an intensity dimension — a least-misery-leaning floor protects the partner with the stronger comfort/escape motive from cognitively or emotionally demanding picks, while averaging suits pairs whose motives are close; (c) divergent motives (one partner escape-driven, the other enrichment-driven) flag genuine conflict that pure trait-similarity would miss, and surface the recommendation as a deliberate compromise (e.g. an emotionally rich but accessible title). The standard group-recommender strategies (average, least-misery, intersection, most-pleasure) all apply, but the presence of an explicit social-motive measurement is the distinctive lever: weight the joint recommendation toward content high on the dimension BOTH partners are motivated by, and use the social-motive level to decide how much content-fit compromise the pair will tolerate. Which strategy maximises joint satisfaction is an untested hypothesis to A/B validate, not an evidence-backed default.
quiz_ux_implications: Better suited to a consumer onboarding quiz than abstract trait scales, because motive items are naturally media-framed and intuitive ('I watch to switch off and relax', 'I watch to learn about the world', 'I watch with someone so we can talk about it'). Biases: (1) Social-desirability is the sharpest concern — escape, coping, and habit/pass-time motives are mildly self-deprecating ('I watch to avoid my problems', 'I watch out of habit') while enrichment/information motives are flattering, so direct Likert self-report will inflate enrichment and deflate escape/coping. The WTSMQ coping-escapism factor is the most desirability-sensitive. (2) Acquiescence bias applies to agreement-format items. (3) State/mood contamination: a user answering after a hard day over-reports escape; the quiz should either fix the frame ('in general, when you pick something to watch...') or deliberately use a current-mood frame and treat it as such. (4) Faking is low-stakes here. Recommendation: disguise via media-framed forced-choice scenarios that pit two equally-legitimate motives against each other, removing the desirability gradient — e.g. 'Friday night, you reach for: something that makes you forget the week / something that gives you a lot to think about'; 'The best thing about a show is: getting completely swept up emotionally / having someone to talk about it with / learning something new / switching your brain off'. Forced-choice between positively-framed alternatives neutralises both acquiescence and the escape-vs-enrichment desirability asymmetry far better than agree-disagree Likert, and reads as part of the fun rather than a psychology test. 5-6 such scenario items recover most of the motive signal in under 90 seconds.
cross_cultural_validity: This is the standout strength of the MUMS family for HelpME2C, and the single best reason to prefer a motive instrument over most trait scales. The WTSMQ (and companion BWESQ) underwent a large cross-cultural psychometric evaluation (Flayelle et al., 2020, Computers in Human Behavior) across NINE languages — English, French, Spanish, Italian, German, Hungarian, Persian, Arabic, and Chinese — in a multinational sample of 12,616 TV-series viewers spanning Africa, Asia, Europe, the Middle East, North America, Oceania, and South America, with confirmatory factor analysis and measurement-invariance testing demonstrating good fit and structural/construct validity in each language. This directly covers several of HelpME2C's harder target locales (Chinese, Arabic, French, German, Spanish, Italian) that trait scales like the NCS/NAQ lacked — a genuine multilingual anchor rather than English-plus-one-European-language. Remaining gaps: (a) Korean, Japanese, Hindi, and Portuguese were NOT in the nine-language WTSMQ set and need separate validation; (b) the Rubin TV motives scale and the Emotion Gratification Scale do not have comparable multilingual invariance evidence (the EGS study was a single Portuguese-language sample); (c) SNS-motive scales (SMU-SNS validated in Spanish, MOTUS, SUMS-R) have narrower language coverage; (d) the escape/coping and social motives are culturally loaded (collectivist vs. individualist norms shape the social motive; emotion-display norms shape emotional-enhancement reporting), so scalar comparability across locales should be assumed only where invariance was actually tested. Net: the WTSMQ provides the best cross-cultural footing of any instrument in this research set, but treat as within-user relative signal outside the nine validated languages.
privacy_consent_implications: Lower sensitivity than trait/personality profiling, but not negligible. Motive data ('why you watch') is personal data and engages GDPR profiling transparency, but is generally less likely than a labelled personality profile to be construed as Article 9 special-category data — 'I watch to relax/learn/escape' is closer to a stated preference than to an inference about health, beliefs, or politics. The exception is the coping-escapism motive: a strong, persistently high coping/escape signal can proxy for stress, low mood, or avoidant coping, edging toward health-adjacent inference if stored in identifiable labelled form, and the WTSMQ literature explicitly frames coping-escapism as a risk marker for problematic binge-watching. Recommendations: collect with explicit, specific consent framed as 'to learn what you're in the mood for'; store a COARSE derived preference lean (e.g. a comfort-vs-enrichment and solo-vs-social band) rather than raw item responses or a labelled motive profile, to reduce re-identification and avoid implying a clinical inference; never surface an explicit 'your escapism/coping score is high' to the user; apply the project's real-deletion guarantees (ADR-0012). Because motive readings are partly state-dependent, avoid treating a single high coping-escape reading as a durable attribute.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Motives map onto the theme vocabulary along an INTENT axis that is partly orthogonal to, and partly a re-expression of, the trait axes (Need for Affect = emotional intensity, Need for Cognition = interpretive work). (1) Coping-escapism motive -> immersive, transporting, lower-cognitive-load escape content: post-apocalyptic-survival, chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, heist-and-caper, fish-out-of-water, magic-system-cost, alternate-history, first-contact, underdog-rise — high-propulsion worlds to disappear into; also comfort tags (found-family, friendship-tested, workplace-procedural, absurdist-comedy) for the comfort sub-flavour of escape. (2) Emotional-enhancement motive (seeking to feel, intensify, or regulate emotion) -> the emotion/relationship cluster: grief-and-loss, forbidden-love, unrequited-longing, coming-of-age, parent-child-rift, toxic-relationship, loneliness-and-isolation, redemption-arc, betrayal-and-forgiveness, plus arousal/thrill tags existential-horror, folk-horror, cosmic-horror, vengeance-and-its-cost (this motive is the proximal cause that Need for Affect is the distal trait for). (3) Enrichment motive (learning, perspective, contemplation, eudaimonic meaning) -> the cognitively and thematically demanding cluster: nonlinear-storytelling, slow-burn, character-study, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, magical-realism, biographical-portrait, ethical-dilemma, moral-compromise, impossible-choice, propaganda-and-truth, satire-of-power, class-conflict, institutional-failure, legacy-of-empire, war-and-its-toll, generational-trauma (this is the proximal cause that Need for Cognition / Openness is the distal trait for). (4) Social / co-viewing motive -> shared-experience-friendly, discussable, broadly-accessible content: mystery-investigation, whodunit, ensemble-mosaic, dark-comedy, satire-of-power, single-incident-fallout — titles that generate conversation; this motive is the most HelpME2C-relevant because it is the one that predicts JOINT watching specifically. (5) Information motive -> factual/biographical/issue-driven: biographical-portrait, corruption-and-cover-up, propaganda-and-truth. The intent framing improves on traits by capturing that the SAME tag (e.g. existential-horror) can serve an emotional-enhancement viewer (wants the dread) or an enrichment viewer (wants the existential idea) — letting the recommender match not just the tag but the reason.
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE (partial) — adopt the MOTIVE FRAMING and a short purpose-built motive battery, but do NOT deploy any full off-the-shelf scale verbatim. Rationale: motives sit causally closer to the chosen title than traits, carry the best cross-cultural validation in this research set (WTSMQ across nine languages incl. Chinese/Arabic), and uniquely measure the social/co-viewing motive that is the direct currency of HelpME2C's joint-watch North Star metric. Concrete proposal: 5-6 disguised, media-framed forced-choice scenario items in the first onboarding pass, each anchored to one high-value motive — (a) comfort/relaxation, (b) escape/transportation, (c) emotional enhancement, (d) enrichment/contemplation, (e) social/co-viewing, (f) information/learning — pitting motives against each other rather than agree-disagree Likert to neutralise the escape-vs-enrichment desirability asymmetry. Use the result as a primary re-ranking lean on the tone/intent axis (comfort<->intensity, escapist<->enriching) and, for two-person mode, weight the joint recommendation toward the dimension both partners share and use the social-motive level to set compromise tolerance. Frame items as 'what are you in the mood for' to embrace, not fight, the state-dependence. Dependencies and flags: (1) Strong overlap with Need for Affect (emotional-enhancement motive) and Need for Cognition (enrichment motive) — if those trait modules are also included, the motive items are partly redundant, so EITHER use motives as the consumer-facing front-end (more intuitive, better cross-cultural) and drop the abstract trait scales, OR keep traits for stable profiling and motives for session-level mood context; do not double-charge the user for both abstractly. RECOMMENDED: prefer motives as the onboarding front-end given their intuitiveness and cross-cultural validation, treating Need for Affect/Cognition as their latent backbone. (2) Treat as a soft, behaviour-correctable prior, not a fixed profile, because motives are state-dependent. (3) Build and lightly validate the 6-item short form — no validated cross-medium short form exists. (4) Store only a coarse derived lean; guard the coping-escapism dimension for the mental-health-adjacent inference risk. (5) The two-person aggregation weighting (amplify shared motive, least-misery floor on intensity/escape divergence) is an untested hypothesis to A/B validate.
key_sources: Rubin, A. M. (1983), 'Television Uses and Gratifications: The Interactions of Viewing Patterns and Motivations', Journal of Broadcasting 27(1):37-51 (foundational 27-item TV motives scale; paywalled): https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/08838158309386471 . Flayelle, M., et al. (2019), 'Assessing binge-watching behaviors: Development and validation of the Watching TV Series Motives (WTSMQ) and Binge-Watching Engagement and Symptoms questionnaires', Computers in Human Behavior 90:26-36 (22-item WTSMQ, 4 factors; paywalled, English item version free): https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563218303972 ; free English WTSMQ items: https://carlalabunil.ch/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/WTSMQ_English.pdf . Flayelle, M., et al. (2020), 'Towards a cross-cultural assessment of binge-watching: Psychometric evaluation of the WTSMQ and BWESQ across nine languages' (N=12,616, 9 languages, invariance; paywalled): https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563220301631 ; RG copy: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/341072851 . Emotion Gratification Scale / 'Why Do We Watch? The Role of Emotion Gratifications and Individual Differences in Predicting Rewatchability and Movie Recommendation', Frontiers in Psychology 2020, N=318 (FREE full text): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7016603/ . Pertegal, Oliva et al. (2019), 'Development and validation of the Scale of Motives for Using Social Networking Sites (SMU-SNS)', PLOS ONE 14(12):e0225781 (27 items, 9 factors; FREE): https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0225781 . MOTUS scale validation (six motives; ScienceDirect 2023): https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503023000403 . SUMS-R (Social Network Site Use Motives Scale-Revised, 8 factors; FREE PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11037477/ . Uses and gratifications theory overview (FREE): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uses_and_gratifications_theory .
